paper.title: A single-cell transcriptomic atlas characterizes ageing tissues in the mouse

glossary.Tabula Muris Senis: The Mouse Ageing Cell Atlas; a single-cell transcriptomic atlas across the lifespan of Mus musculus covering 23 tissues and organs at six age groups
glossary.Tabula Muris: The previously published single-cell transcriptomic atlas of 20 mouse organs at 3 months of age, used as a reference for cell-type annotation
glossary.FACS: Fluorescence-activated cell sorting; here used to sort single cells into microtitre well plates for full-length transcript sequencing via Smart-seq2
glossary.droplet: Microfluidic droplet-based single-cell RNA sequencing using the 10x Genomics Chromium platform for 3-prime end counting
glossary.Cdkn2a: Gene encoding the p16 tumor suppressor protein; a widely used marker of cellular senescence
glossary.Sirt: Sirtuin gene family; NAD-dependent deacetylases implicated in delaying cellular senescence
glossary.Umod: Gene encoding uromodulin, the most abundant protein in urine, produced by epithelial cells lining the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle
glossary.Mrc1: Mannose receptor C-type 1 gene; encodes the mannose receptor involved in immune defense
glossary.LSECs: Liver sinusoidal endothelial cells; specialized endothelial cells with a unique role in hepatic immune defense
glossary.ERCC: External RNA Controls Consortium spike-in RNA standards used to estimate technical error rates in sequencing
glossary.MHC: Major histocompatibility complex; a set of cell surface proteins essential for adaptive immune system recognition
glossary.AP1: Activator protein 1 transcription factor complex, including Jun and Fos family members
glossary.Jchain: Gene encoding the joining chain of immunoglobulins, also known as Igj; a marker of plasma cells
glossary.bbknn: Batch balanced k-nearest neighbours; a batch-alignment algorithm used to correct for method-associated batch effects
glossary.MAST: Model-based Analysis of Single-cell Transcriptomics; a statistical framework for differential expression analysis treating age as a continuous covariate
glossary.TraCeR: Computational tool for reconstructing T cell receptor sequences from single-cell RNA-seq data
glossary.singlecell-ige: Computational tool for reconstructing B cell receptor sequences from single-cell RNA-seq data
glossary.PAGA: Partition-based graph abstraction; a method for reconstructing developmental or ageing trajectories from single-cell data
glossary.diversity score: Shannon entropy of cluster assignments for a given cell type, regressed against age to detect systematic changes in transcriptomic heterogeneity
glossary.clonotype: A group of B cells or T cells sharing the same receptor sequence, indicating clonal expansion from a common ancestor

nodes.claim_text: Single-cell transcriptomic profiling across the mouse lifespan reveals cell-type-specific molecular changes and tissue compositional shifts that characterize ageing across multiple organs.
nodes.evidence_location: Abstract; Title; Discussion (page 594)
nodes.supporting_quote: Using single-cell transcriptomic data, we assessed cell-type-specific manifestations of different hallmarks of ageing-such as senescence, genomic instability and changes in the immune system.

nodes.claim_text: The Tabula Muris Senis provides a comprehensive single-cell transcriptomic resource that reveals how the most important hallmarks of ageing are reflected across a broad range of tissues and cell types.
nodes.evidence_location: Abstract; Discussion (page 594)
nodes.supporting_quote: This transcriptomic atlas-which we denote Tabula Muris Senis, or 'Mouse Ageing Cell Atlas'-provides molecular information about how the most important hallmarks of ageing are reflected in a broad range of tissues and cell types.

nodes.claim_text: The Tabula Muris Senis is a validated, manually curated single-cell transcriptomic atlas spanning 23 tissues across six age groups, enabling cell-type-specific analyses of ageing.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA-seq (FACS Smart-seq2 and 10x Genomics droplet)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on more than 350,000 cells from male and female C57BL/6JN mice at six age groups (1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months) across 23 tissues using both FACS-based Smart-seq2 and 10x Genomics Chromium droplet platforms. After quality filtering, 110,824 FACS cells and 245,389 droplet cells were retained.
nodes.analysis_type: cell type annotation and cross-method validation
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Single-cell RNA-seq data from 23 tissues across 6 age groups (1, 3, 18, 21, 24, 30 months) collected via two methods: FACS (Smart-seq2, full-length transcripts, 384-well plates) and microfluidic droplet (10x Genomics, 3-prime end counting). A total of 529,823 cells were sequenced; 110,824 FACS cells and 245,389 droplet cells passed filtering.

PREPROCESSING: Gene count tables were combined with metadata using Scanpy v1.4.2. Genes not expressed in at least 3 cells were removed. Cells with fewer than 250 detected genes were removed. For FACS, cells with fewer than 5,000 counts were removed; for droplet, cells with fewer than 2,500 UMIs were removed. Data was normalized using size factor normalization (10,000 counts per cell) and log-transformed. Highly variable genes were computed using default Scanpy parameters. Data was scaled to a maximum value of 10. PCA was computed, followed by neighbourhood graph construction.

CORE ANALYSIS - Cell type annotation: Each organ was analyzed independently across all ages. PCA was performed on the most variable genes, followed by Louvain (Blondel et al. 2008) and Leiden (Traag et al. 2019) graph-based clustering. The 3-month Tabula Muris data was subset from each organ's clustered data, and the mapping between existing Tabula Muris cell-type labels and clusters was assessed. For clusters with a single 1:1 mapping (one cluster to one cell type), annotations were propagated to cells of all ages in that cluster. Clusters with 1:many mappings were flagged for manual validation. All automated annotations were reviewed and approved by tissue expert curators; remaining unannotated cells were annotated manually. Annotations were provided in the controlled vocabulary of the Cell Ontology.

CORE ANALYSIS - Cross-method validation: To assess annotation consistency across the entire organism, the bbknn batch-alignment algorithm (Polanski et al. 2020) was used in place of the standard neighbourhood graph computation to correct for method-associated batch effects (FACS vs droplet). After batch correction, all cells were clustered using Louvain graph-based clustering. The co-occurrence of similarly annotated cells (e.g., B cells, endothelial cells) in the same clusters was assessed irrespective of tissue of origin or processing method.

OUTPUT: A fully curated cell-type annotation for all 356,213 cells passing QC, with annotations consistent across tissues and methods as validated by cross-organism clustering. More than 70% of cells were automatically annotated; the remainder were manually curated.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 1; Main text paragraphs 1-2 (page 590)
nodes.supporting_quote: We performed single-cell RNA sequencing on more than 350,000 cells from male and female C57BL/6JN mice belonging to six age groups, ranging from 1 month (the equivalent of human early childhood) to 30 months (the equivalent of a human centenarian).

nodes.claim_text: Cellular senescence markers, particularly Cdkn2a, show age-related increases in both the fraction of expressing cells and per-cell expression levels across the organism.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 2a-d; Main text paragraph 3 (page 590-591); Supplementary Table 4
nodes.supporting_quote: The expression of Cdkn2a and its protein product p16 is one of the most frequently used markers of senescence and is an important hallmark of ageing.

nodes.claim_text: The cellular composition of tissues changes with age, with distinct cell-type proportional shifts observed in the bladder, kidney, spleen, mammary gland, liver, bone marrow, and skin.
nodes.evidence_location: Figures 2e-j; Extended Data Figures 5-6; Main text (pages 591-593)
nodes.supporting_quote: The cellular composition of each tissue type tends to vary with age.

nodes.claim_text: Genomic instability, as measured by somatic mutation accumulation in transcribed genes, increases with age across all analysed organs and cell types.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 3; Extended Data Figures 7-8; Main text (page 593)
nodes.supporting_quote: Genomic instability is among the most widely studied hallmarks of ageing, and full-length transcript data enables analysis of the accumulation of somatic mutations with age.

nodes.claim_text: The adaptive immune system shows age-related changes including increased clonality of both B cell and T cell repertoires, suggesting reduced capacity to respond to new pathogens.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 4a-b; Extended Data Figure 9; Main text (pages 593-594)
nodes.supporting_quote: These changes in clonality for both B cell and T cell repertoires are noteworthy, because they suggest that the immune system of a 24-month-old mouse is less likely to respond to new pathogens.

nodes.claim_text: Immune cell populations in the brain and kidney undergo significant age-related transcriptomic diversification, with microglia acquiring a pro-inflammatory and neurodegeneration-associated signature and kidney macrophages shifting from an anti-inflammatory to a pro-inflammatory state.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 4c-f; Extended Data Figures 10-11; Main text (pages 593-594)
nodes.supporting_quote: We observed significant changes in diversity for cells of the immune system that originate from the brain and the kidney (Fig. 4c, Extended Data Fig. 11a, b).

nodes.claim_text: Single-cell data resolves that bulk gene expression changes with age are often attributable to both changes in cell-type proportions and changes in per-cell gene expression levels.
nodes.evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 5a-b; Main text paragraph 3 (page 590)
nodes.supporting_quote: we observed that-in many cases-changes in gene expression are due to both changes in the numbers of cells in a population and changes in the gene expression levels in each cell

nodes.claim_text: The bladder undergoes pronounced age-related changes in cellular composition and gene expression, with a shift from mesenchymal to urothelial dominance, declining vascularization, and increasing inflammatory signatures.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 2e-f; Main text (page 591); Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: The bladder shows pronounced changes in cell-type composition with age (Fig. 2e)

nodes.claim_text: Age-related changes in the kidney include declining glomerular and tubular cell populations with compensatory increases in local gene expression, suggesting impaired kidney function.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 2g-h; Main text (pages 591-592); Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: Both mesangial cells and capillary endothelial cells are core glomerular cells, and the reduction in their relative abundances with age (Fig. 2g, top)-together with a tissue-wide reduction of Egf and Atp1a1 expression (Fig. 2h, Supplementary Table 6)-suggest an impaired glomerular filtration rate

nodes.claim_text: The spleen shows age-related shifts in immune cell composition, with declining T cells and increasing plasma cells.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 2i-j; Main text (page 592); Supplementary Table 5-6
nodes.supporting_quote: In the spleen, the proportion of T cells decreases with age while the relative amount of plasma cells increases (Fig. 2i).

nodes.claim_text: The liver undergoes age-related changes including declining hepatocyte proportions, increased immune gene signatures, and altered Kupffer cell and sinusoidal endothelial cell phenotypes.
nodes.evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 6; Main text (page 593); Supplementary Tables 6-7
nodes.supporting_quote: The tissue composition of the liver also changes with age.

nodes.claim_text: The mammary gland shows age-related T cell decline and upregulation of AP1 transcription factor genes.
nodes.evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 5c-d; Main text (pages 592-593); Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: Similarly, in the mammary gland we observed a decline of the T cell population (Extended Data Fig. 5c).

nodes.claim_text: B cell clonality increases progressively with age across the organism.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 4a; Main text (pages 593-594); Supplementary Table 9
nodes.supporting_quote: whereas most of the cells at 3 months were not part of a clone (9% were part of a clonal family), the number of B cells belonging to a clonotype doubled at 18 months (20%) when compared to 3 months, and doubled again from 18 months to 24 months (around 38%).

nodes.claim_text: T cell clonality increases markedly with age across the organism.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 4b; Main text (pages 593-594); Supplementary Table 9
nodes.supporting_quote: only around 3% (55 out of 1,895) of the cells at 3 months were part of a clone. At 18 months and 24 months, this value increased to around 23% (479 out of 2,056) and around 20% (348 out of 1,780) of the cells, respectively

nodes.claim_text: Brain microglia undergo age-related transcriptomic changes characterized by expansion of a pro-inflammatory subset with interferon-responsive and neurodegeneration-associated gene signatures.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 4c-e; Extended Data Figure 11a,c,e; Main text (page 594); Supplementary Table 10
nodes.supporting_quote: gene expression in clusters 10, 12 and 14 is enriched with interferon responsive or regulatory genes (for example, Oasl2, Oas1a, Ifit3, Rtp4, Bst2, Stat1, Irf7, Ifitm3, Usp18, Ifi204 and Ifit2), suggesting an expansion of this small pro-inflammatory subset of microglia in the ageing brain

nodes.claim_text: Kidney macrophages shift from an anti-inflammatory (M2) to a pro-inflammatory (M1) transcriptomic state with age.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 4f; Extended Data Figure 11b,d,f; Main text (page 594); Supplementary Table 10
nodes.supporting_quote: Differential gene expression revealed that cluster 10 is enriched for an M2-macrophage gene signature (for example, Il10, H2-Eb1, H2-Ab1, H2-Aa, Cd74, C1qa, Cxcl16, Hexb, Cd81, C1qb and Cd72) whereas cluster 13 resembles a M1-proinflammatory macrophage state (for example, Hp, Itgal, Msrb1 and Gngt2)

nodes.claim_text: Cdkn2a expression increases with age in both the fraction of expressing cells and per-cell expression levels, with the strongest age-correlation among senescence markers.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 2a-d; Main text paragraph 3 (page 590-591); Supplementary Table 4
nodes.supporting_quote: Cdkn2a has the highest correlation between ageing and the fraction of cells in which it is expressed

nodes.claim_text: The fraction of cells expressing sirtuin family genes decreases with age, consistent with their role in delaying cellular senescence.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text paragraph 3 (page 591); Supplementary Table 4
nodes.supporting_quote: For some genes, including members of the Sirt family (Sirt3, Sirt4 and Sirt5), the fraction of cells in which they were expressed was found to decrease with age; this is consistent with previous literature finding that sirtuins-the protein products of Sirt genes-are essential in delaying cellular senescence

nodes.claim_text: Cdkn2a and its protein product p16 is one of the most frequently used markers of senescence and is an important hallmark of ageing (Rayess et al. 2012; Hernandez-Segura et al. 2018).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text paragraph 3 (page 590-591); citing refs 10, 11
nodes.supporting_quote: The expression of Cdkn2a and its protein product p16 is one of the most frequently used markers of senescence and is an important hallmark of ageing.

nodes.claim_text: Sirtuins are essential in delaying cellular senescence (Saunders & Verdin 2007; Lee et al. 2019).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text paragraph 3 (page 591); citing refs 20, 21
nodes.supporting_quote: this is consistent with previous literature finding that sirtuins-the protein products of Sirt genes-are essential in delaying cellular senescence

nodes.claim_text: Age-related decline of T cell populations has been associated with increased risk of infectious disease and cancer (Palmer et al. 2018).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text (page 592); citing ref 33
nodes.supporting_quote: Age-related decline of T cell populations has been associated with increased risk of infectious disease and cancer

nodes.claim_text: Normal mammary gland involution is accompanied by increased expression of AP1 transcription factor family genes (Shen et al. 2006; Girnius et al. 2018).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text (pages 592-593); citing refs 34, 35
nodes.supporting_quote: consistent with the observation that normal involution of the mammary gland is accompanied by increased expression of this gene family

nodes.claim_text: Pro-inflammatory macrophages drive cellular senescence, and Il1b shows markedly different expression in the liver with age (Covarrubias et al. 2019).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text (page 593); citing ref 12
nodes.supporting_quote: Previous findings suggested that pro-inflammatory macrophages drive cellular senescence, and identified the gene Il1b as showing markedly different expression in the liver with age

nodes.claim_text: Specific blocking of IL1-RI in hepatocytes attenuates cell death after injury, supporting the idea that increased Il1b expression in Kupffer cells is a poor prognostic indicator (Gehrke et al. 2018).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text (page 593); citing ref 37
nodes.supporting_quote: Specific blocking of IL1-RI in hepatocytes has been shown to attenuate cell death after injury, supporting the idea that increased expression of Il1b in Kupffer cells is typically a poor prognostic

nodes.claim_text: Liver sinusoidal endothelial cells are the main carriers of the mannose receptor (MRC1) in the liver (Liu et al. 2013).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text (page 593); citing ref 38
nodes.supporting_quote: Liver sinusoidal endothelial cells (LSECs) have a unique role in immune defence, being the main carriers of the mannose receptor (MRC1) in this organ

nodes.claim_text: A unique microglia type associated with restricting development of Alzheimer's disease has been identified with a specific transcriptomic signature (Keren-Shaul et al. 2017).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text (page 594); citing ref 47
nodes.supporting_quote: the list of differentially expressed genes between 'young' and 'old' clusters resembles the previously reported Alzheimer's disease-specific microglial signature

nodes.claim_text: Older individuals have higher vulnerability to new infections and lower benefits from vaccination compared with younger individuals (Goronzy & Weyand 2013, 2017).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text (page 594); citing refs 45, 46
nodes.supporting_quote: This corroborates literature reports suggesting that older individuals have a higher vulnerability to new infections and lower benefits from vaccination compared with younger individuals

nodes.claim_text: Downregulation of ATP1A1 is associated with impaired function in renal cell carcinoma, and EGF serves as a prognostic biomarker in chronic kidney diseases (Zhang et al. 2017; Isaka 2016).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text (pages 591-592); citing refs 29, 30
nodes.supporting_quote: together with a tissue-wide reduction of Egf and Atp1a1 expression (Fig. 2h, Supplementary Table 6)-suggest an impaired glomerular filtration rate

nodes.claim_text: M1 and M2 macrophage states are defined by distinct marker gene sets (Jablonski et al. 2015).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text (page 594); citing ref 50
nodes.supporting_quote: cluster 13 resembles a M1-proinflammatory macrophage state

nodes.claim_text: The proportion of cells expressing Cdkn2a more than doubled in older mice compared with younger mice in both FACS and droplet datasets.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA-seq (FACS Smart-seq2 and 10x Genomics droplet)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA-seq was performed on cells from C57BL/6JN mice at six age groups (1, 3, 18, 21, 24, 30 months) across 23 tissues using both FACS-based Smart-seq2 and 10x Genomics droplet platforms. n = 44,518, 34,027 and 31,551 cells for FACS at 3, 18 and 24 months; n = 25,980, 45,602, 44,645, 35,828, 37,660 and 55,674 cells for droplet at 1, 3, 18, 21, 24 and 30 months.
nodes.analysis_type: fraction of cells expressing a marker gene across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: All cells passing QC filters from the FACS dataset (n = 44,518 at 3 months, 34,027 at 18 months, 31,551 at 24 months) and the droplet dataset (n = 25,980 at 1 month, 45,602 at 3 months, 44,645 at 18 months, 35,828 at 21 months, 37,660 at 24 months, 55,674 at 30 months), analyzed separately by method.

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing as described for the full dataset: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, FACS cells with fewer than 5,000 counts removed, droplet cells with fewer than 2,500 UMIs removed. Data normalized to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformed.

CORE ANALYSIS: For each age group, the fraction of all cells expressing Cdkn2a was computed, where a cell is considered to express Cdkn2a if it has greater than 0 counts for that gene. The fraction was computed as (number of cells with Cdkn2a > 0) / (total number of cells in that age group). Pairwise comparisons between age groups were performed using the Mann-Whitney-Wilcoxon rank-sum two-sided test. Analysis was performed separately for FACS (3 age groups: 3, 18, 24 months) and droplet (6 age groups: 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, 30 months).

OUTPUT: Bar plots showing the fraction of cells expressing Cdkn2a per age group for each method, with exact P values from pairwise Mann-Whitney-Wilcoxon tests. Data expressed as mean +/- s.d. with individual data points shown.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 2a-d; Main text - paragraph 3 (page 590-591)
nodes.supporting_quote: The proportion of cells expressing Cdkn2a more than doubled in older mice compared with younger mice according to analysis by both FACS (Fig. 2a) and droplet (Fig. 2b) methods; this was accompanied by a twofold increase in the expression levels of p16 in cells in which it was expressed (Fig. 2c, d).

nodes.claim_text: The mean expression level of Cdkn2a approximately doubled in cells that express it when comparing old to young mice in both FACS and droplet data.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA-seq (FACS Smart-seq2 and 10x Genomics droplet)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA-seq was performed on cells from C57BL/6JN mice at six age groups across 23 tissues using both FACS and droplet platforms. Same sample sizes as L1.
nodes.analysis_type: per-cell expression level comparison across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Subset of cells that express Cdkn2a (>0 counts) from the FACS dataset (3, 18, 24 months) and the droplet dataset (1, 3, 18, 21, 24, 30 months). Total cell numbers per age group are the same as in L1.

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing: normalization to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformation. Only cells with Cdkn2a expression > 0 (after normalization and log-transformation) were retained for this analysis.

CORE ANALYSIS: For each age group, the median expression level of Cdkn2a was computed across only those cells that express the gene (>0 counts). Expression values are log-transformed and scaled. Pairwise comparisons between age groups were performed using the Mann-Whitney-Wilcoxon rank-sum two-sided test. Analysis was performed separately for FACS and droplet datasets.

OUTPUT: Bar plots of the median expression of Cdkn2a for expressing cells at each age group, with y-axis showing log-transformed and scaled values. Data expressed as mean +/- s.d. with individual data points shown. Exact P values from pairwise Mann-Whitney-Wilcoxon tests are reported.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 2c-d; Main text - paragraph 3 (page 590-591)
nodes.supporting_quote: this was accompanied by a twofold increase in the expression levels of p16 in cells in which it was expressed (Fig. 2c, d).

nodes.claim_text: In 30-month-old mice the fraction of cells expressing Cdkn2a was smaller than in 24-month-old mice.
nodes.experiment_detail: Droplet-based single-cell RNA-seq on cells from C57BL/6JN mice at 24 months (n = 37,660 cells) and 30 months (n = 55,674 cells).
nodes.analysis_type: fraction of cells expressing a marker gene across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: All cells passing QC filters from the droplet dataset at 24 months (n = 37,660) and 30 months (n = 55,674).

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, cells with fewer than 2,500 UMIs removed. Data normalized to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformed.

CORE ANALYSIS: For each of the two age groups (24 months and 30 months), the fraction of all cells expressing Cdkn2a was computed, where a cell is considered to express Cdkn2a if it has greater than 0 counts for that gene. The fractions were compared between the two age groups. This comparison is part of the broader droplet analysis across all 6 age groups (1, 3, 18, 21, 24, 30 months) shown in Figure 2b.

OUTPUT: The fraction of cells expressing Cdkn2a at 24 months and 30 months, enabling direct comparison of these two age groups within the droplet dataset.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 2b; Main text - paragraph 3 (page 590-591)
nodes.supporting_quote: Notably, in 30-month-old mice the fraction of cells that expressed p16 was smaller than in 24-month-old mice, perhaps because long-living animals have a slower rate of senescence.

nodes.claim_text: Among previously characterized senescence markers, Cdkn2a showed the highest correlation between ageing and the fraction of cells in which it is expressed.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA-seq (FACS Smart-seq2 and 10x Genomics droplet)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA-seq data from all age groups across all tissues. A curated list of previously characterized senescence markers was used (Supplementary Table 4).
nodes.analysis_type: correlation of senescence marker expression fraction with age
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: All cells passing QC filters from both FACS and droplet datasets across all age groups. A curated list of previously characterized senescence markers compiled from references 12-15 (provided in Supplementary Table 4).

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, method-specific count thresholds applied (5,000 for FACS, 2,500 UMIs for droplet). Data normalized to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformed.

CORE ANALYSIS: For each gene in the curated senescence marker list (Supplementary Table 4), the fraction of cells expressing the gene (>0 counts) was computed per age group across all tissues. The correlation between age (treated as a numerical variable corresponding to the age groups) and the fraction of cells expressing each senescence marker was computed. Genes were ranked by their correlation coefficient.

OUTPUT: A ranking of senescence markers by their correlation between age and fraction of expressing cells. Cdkn2a was identified as having the highest positive correlation. The full results are provided in Supplementary Table 4.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text - paragraph 3 (page 591); Supplementary Table 4
nodes.supporting_quote: Cdkn2a has the highest correlation between ageing and the fraction of cells in which it is expressed; other genes with positive correlation include E2f2, Lmnb1, Tnf and Itgax.

nodes.claim_text: The fraction of cells expressing members of the Sirt family (Sirt3, Sirt4, Sirt5) decreased with age.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA-seq (FACS Smart-seq2 and 10x Genomics droplet)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA-seq data from all age groups across all tissues.
nodes.analysis_type: fraction of cells expressing senescence-associated genes across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: All cells passing QC filters from both FACS and droplet datasets across all age groups. Sirt3, Sirt4, and Sirt5 were included in the curated list of senescence markers (Supplementary Table 4).

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, method-specific count thresholds applied (5,000 for FACS, 2,500 UMIs for droplet). Data normalized to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformed.

CORE ANALYSIS: For each of Sirt3, Sirt4, and Sirt5, the fraction of cells expressing the gene (>0 counts) was computed per age group across all tissues. The correlation between age and the fraction of cells expressing each gene was computed, as part of the broader senescence marker analysis described in Supplementary Table 4.

OUTPUT: The fraction of cells expressing Sirt3, Sirt4, and Sirt5 at each age group, along with the correlation between age and expression fraction for each gene.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text - paragraph 3 (page 591); Supplementary Table 4
nodes.supporting_quote: For some genes, including members of the Sirt family (Sirt3, Sirt4 and Sirt5), the fraction of cells in which they were expressed was found to decrease with age

nodes.claim_text: In the bladder, the mesenchymal cell compartment decreased by approximately threefold over the mouse lifespan while the urothelial compartment increased by a similar amount.
nodes.experiment_detail: Droplet-based single-cell RNA-seq on bladder cells from C57BL/6JN mice at 1, 3, 18, and 24 months. n = 970, 3,804, 2,739 and 3,864 cells respectively.
nodes.analysis_type: cell type proportion analysis across age
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: All cells passing QC filters from the bladder across available time points (n = 970 at 1 month, 3,804 at 3 months, 2,739 at 18 months, 3,864 at 24 months), with cell-type annotations assigned during the annotation workflow.

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing applied to the bladder dataset: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, cells with fewer than 2,500 UMIs removed (droplet). Data normalized to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformed.

CORE ANALYSIS: For each age group, the relative proportion of each cell type in the bladder was computed as (number of cells of a given type) / (total number of cells in that tissue at that age). Linear regression of relative cell-type proportion against age was performed using scipy.stats linregress. Significance was assessed with P < 0.05 for a hypothesis test with the null hypothesis that the slope is zero, using a two-sided Wald test with t-distribution of the test statistic, and r2 > 0.7 (the threshold stated in the figure legend for bladder). This was computed separately for bladder mesenchymal cells and bladder urothelial cells.

OUTPUT: Scatter plots of relative cell-type proportion versus age for bladder mesenchymal cells and bladder urothelial cells, with regression lines. P values and r2 values for the linear regression are reported in Supplementary Table 5.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 2e; Main text - bladder composition (page 591)
nodes.supporting_quote: whereas the mesenchymal compartment of this tissue decreases by a factor of three over the lifetime of the mouse (Fig. 2e, left), the urothelial compartment increases by a similar amount (Fig. 2e, right).

nodes.claim_text: Differential gene expression analysis of the bladder showed downregulation of stromal-associated genes (Col1a1, Col1a2, Col3a1, Dcn) and upregulation of epithelial-associated genes (Krt15, Krt18, Sfn) with age.
nodes.experiment_detail: Droplet-based single-cell RNA-seq on bladder cells from C57BL/6JN mice at 1, 3, 18, and 24 months. n = 970, 3,804, 2,739 and 3,864 cells respectively.
nodes.analysis_type: differential gene expression analysis with age as continuous covariate
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: All cells passing QC filters from the bladder across available time points (n = 970 at 1 month, 3,804 at 3 months, 2,739 at 18 months, 3,864 at 24 months).

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, cells with fewer than 2,500 UMIs removed (droplet). Data normalized to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformed.

CORE ANALYSIS: Differential gene expression analysis was performed on the bladder tissue using MAST (Finak et al. 2015), a flexible statistical framework for single-cell data. Age was treated as a continuous numerical covariate while controlling for sex and technology (FACS vs droplet) as additional covariates in the linear model. Genes were classified as significantly differentially expressed under a false-discovery rate (FDR) threshold of 0.01 and an age coefficient threshold of 0.005 (corresponding to an approximately 10% fold change).

OUTPUT: A ranked list of differentially expressed genes with age coefficients. The top 20 upregulated and top 20 downregulated genes are shown in Figure 2f. The full gene list is provided in Supplementary Table 6.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 2f; Main text - bladder composition (page 591); Supplementary Table 6

nodes.claim_text: The number of Clec4f-positive Kupffer cells in the liver did not change with age, but the number of cells co-expressing Clec4f and Il1b increased with age.
nodes.experiment_detail: In situ RNA hybridization was performed on 5-micrometer paraffin-embedded liver sections from C57BL/6JN mice at 3, 12, and 30 months using RNAscope Multiplex Fluorescent Detection kit v2. Probes for Clec4f and Il1b were used. Quantification was performed using the starfish image-based transcriptomics pipeline.
nodes.analysis_type: in situ quantification of co-expressing cells across age
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Paraffin-embedded mouse liver sections (5 micrometers thick) from mice at 3, 12, and 30 months of age. Mouse livers were fixed in 10% formalin buffer saline for 24 hours at room temperature before paraffin embedding.

PREPROCESSING: Sections were prepared according to the Advanced Cell Diagnostics RNAscope Multiplex Fluorescent Detection kit v2 (323110, Bio-Techne) manufacturer's instructions.

CORE ANALYSIS: In situ RNA hybridization was performed using RNAscope with multiplex probes for Clec4f (Mm-Clec4f 480421) and Il1b (Mm-Il1b 316891-C2). Slides were counterstained with Prolong gold antifade reagent with DAPI (P36931, Life Technologies). Mounted slides were imaged on a Leica DM6 B fluorescent microscope. Image quantification was performed using the starfish open source image-based transcriptomics pipeline. The number of Clec4f-positive cells and the number of cells co-expressing Clec4f and Il1b were quantified per field of view at each age. The ratio of Il1b+/Clec4f+ cells was also computed. Statistical comparisons between age groups were performed using Welch's test.

OUTPUT: Quantification of Clec4f+ cells per field of view at 3, 12, and 30 months (Extended Data Figure 6h), Il1b+ cells per field of view (Extended Data Figure 6i), and the ratio of Il1b+/Clec4f+ cells (Extended Data Figure 6j), with P values from Welch's test. Sample sizes per group are in Supplementary Table 7.
nodes.evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 6g-j; Main text - liver composition (page 593); Supplementary Table 7
nodes.supporting_quote: We performed in situ RNA staining in liver Kupffer cells (Extended Data Fig. 6g) with Clec4f and found that the number of Clec4f-positive cells does not change with age, consistent with the results of the tissue composition analysis (Extended Data Fig. 6h, Supplementary Table 7). However, when co-staining with Il1b, we found that the number of cells expressing both Clec4f and Il1b increased with age

nodes.claim_text: An age-related increase in the number of somatic mutations was observed across all analysed organs using full-length transcript data from FACS.
nodes.experiment_detail: Full-length transcript single-cell RNA-seq data from FACS Smart-seq2 across all analysed organs at 3, 18, and 24 months. GATK HaplotypeCaller was used to identify single-nucleotide polymorphisms.
nodes.analysis_type: somatic mutation calling from transcriptomic data
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Aligned BAM files from all FACS (Smart-seq2) samples across 3-month, 18-month, and 24-month time points. The GRCm38 reference genome was used. ERCC spike-in control sequences were included in each well.

PREPROCESSING: samtools v1.9 was used to create the index file (samtools faidx). GATK v4.1.1.0 CreateSequenceDictionary was used with GRCm38 as reference. GATK AddOrReplaceReadGroups was used to create a single read group with parameters: -RGID 4 -RGLB lib1 -RGPL illumina -RGPU unit1 -RGSM 20.

CORE ANALYSIS: GATK HaplotypeCaller was used to call mutations. The following read filters were disabled: MappingQualityReadFilter, GoodCigarReadFilter, NotSecondaryAlignmentReadFilter, MappedReadFilter, MappingQualityAvailableReadFilter, NonzeroReferenceLengthAlignmentReadFilter, NotDuplicateReadFilter, PassesVendorQualityCheckReadFilter, and WellformedReadFilter; all other default settings were kept. Results were summarized per gene as a mutation count per cell table. Genes mutated in more than 60% of cells were removed to eliminate possible germline mutation bias. For each tissue, genes expressed in at least 75% of cells at all time points were selected to avoid confounding mutation results with differential gene expression associated with age. The average number of mutations in the gene set per cell was computed for each age group. The same pipeline was applied to ERCC spike-in control sequences. The pairwise difference of mean mutations between age groups (24 months minus 3 months, and 18 months minus 3 months) was computed for both the gene set and ERCC controls. Average raw counts per gene per cell were also computed to control for sequencing coverage.

OUTPUT: Distributions of the pairwise difference of mean mutations per cell between age groups for the gene set and for ERCC spike-in controls, across all tissues and stratified by cell type. Shown in Figure 3 and Extended Data Figures 7-8. Detailed results in Supplementary Table 8.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 3; Extended Data Figure 7; Main text - genomic instability / mutations (page 593)
nodes.supporting_quote: We focused on genes that were expressed in at least 75% of cells for each age group within a particular tissue, and observed an age-related increase in the number of mutations across all of the organs we analysed (Fig. 3, Extended Data Fig. 7a, c, e)

nodes.claim_text: Tongue and bladder were the most affected organs in terms of age-related accumulation of somatic mutations.
nodes.experiment_detail: Full-length transcript single-cell RNA-seq data from FACS Smart-seq2 across all analysed organs at 3, 18, and 24 months.
nodes.analysis_type: somatic mutation calling from transcriptomic data, cross-organ comparison
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: The mutation count per cell tables generated from the GATK HaplotypeCaller pipeline applied to all FACS samples, as described in L24. Results were available for all tissues with FACS data at 3, 18, and 24 months.

PREPROCESSING: Same as L24: genes mutated in >60% of cells removed; for each tissue, only genes expressed in at least 75% of cells at all time points were retained.

CORE ANALYSIS: The average number of mutations per gene per cell was computed for each tissue and age group. The pairwise difference of mean mutations between age groups (24 months minus 3 months, and 18 months minus 3 months) was computed for each tissue. Tissues were compared by the magnitude of the age-related mutation increase.

OUTPUT: A ranking of tissues by the magnitude of age-related increase in mean mutations per cell. Results shown in Extended Data Figure 7.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text - genomic instability / mutations (page 593); Extended Data Figure 7
nodes.supporting_quote: tongue and bladder were the most affected.

nodes.claim_text: The observed age-related increase in mutations exceeded the level expected from amplification and sequencing errors as estimated using ERCC spike-in controls.
nodes.experiment_detail: Full-length transcript single-cell RNA-seq data from FACS Smart-seq2 with ERCC spike-in controls added to each well.
nodes.analysis_type: comparison of mutation rates in genes vs ERCC spike-in controls
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Mutation calls from GATK HaplotypeCaller applied to both the gene set and ERCC spike-in control sequences from all FACS samples, as described in L24.

PREPROCESSING: Same as L24 for the gene set. For ERCC controls, the same GATK HaplotypeCaller pipeline was applied to ERCC spike-in sequences that were spiked into each well.

CORE ANALYSIS: The pairwise difference of mean mutations between age groups was computed separately for the gene set and for ERCC spike-in controls. For each comparison (24 months minus 3 months, and 18 months minus 3 months), the distribution of mean mutation differences in the gene set was compared to the distribution of mean mutation differences in ERCC controls. The ERCC controls provide an estimate of mutations expected from amplification and sequencing errors alone, since ERCC sequences are synthetic and should not accumulate biological mutations. Additionally, the average raw counts per gene per cell and per ERCC per cell were computed to control for sequencing coverage differences between age groups.

OUTPUT: Overlaid distributions of pairwise mean mutation differences for gene sets (filled distributions) and ERCC controls (white/dashed distributions) shown in Figure 3a-f. Raw expression controls shown in Extended Data Figure 8. The gene set mutation differences exceed ERCC control differences, indicating the age-related increase is above the technical error baseline.

nodes.supporting_quote: we verified that the number of mutations exceeded those expected from amplification and sequencing errors, which can be estimated using External RNA Controls Consortium (ERCC) controls that were spiked into each well

nodes.experiment_detail: Full-length transcript single-cell RNA-seq data from FACS Smart-seq2 for B cells at 3, 18, and 24 months (n = 1,818, 1,356, and 2,876 cells respectively).
nodes.analysis_type: B cell receptor assembly from scRNA-seq
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Full-length transcript FACS (Smart-seq2) data for cells annotated as B cells across all tissues at 3, 18, and 24 months.

PREPROCESSING: No additional preprocessing beyond standard FACS QC filtering (cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, cells with fewer than 5,000 counts removed).

CORE ANALYSIS: singlecell-ige (version eafb6d126cc2d6511faae3efbd442abd7c6dc8ef, available from https://github.com/dcroote/singlecell-ige) was used to computationally reconstruct B cell receptor sequences from full-length FACS transcript data. Default configuration settings were used, except the species was set to mouse. The singlecell-ige output was parsed to define B cell clonotypes based on the assembled B cell receptor sequences.

OUTPUT: B cell receptor sequences assembled for 6,050 cells total: 1,818 cells at 3 months, 1,356 cells at 18 months, and 2,876 cells at 24 months. Clonotype assignments provided in Supplementary Table 9.

nodes.claim_text: The fraction of B cells belonging to a clonal family increased from approximately 9% at 3 months to approximately 20% at 18 months and approximately 38% at 24 months.
nodes.experiment_detail: Full-length transcript single-cell RNA-seq data from FACS Smart-seq2 for B cells at 3, 18, and 24 months (n = 1,818, 1,356, and 2,876 cells respectively).
nodes.analysis_type: B cell clonality analysis
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: B cell receptor assembly output from singlecell-ige for 6,050 B cells across 3 time points (1,818 at 3 months, 1,356 at 18 months, 2,876 at 24 months), as generated in L27.

PREPROCESSING: The singlecell-ige output was parsed to define B cell clonotypes based on assembled B cell receptor sequences.

CORE ANALYSIS: For each age group, cells were classified as either singleton B cells (unique clonotype, not shared with any other cell) or as belonging to a clonal family (clonotype shared with at least one other cell). The fraction of B cells belonging to a clonal family was computed as (number of cells in clonal families) / (total number of cells with assembled BCR) for each age group.

OUTPUT: Pie charts showing the proportion of singleton B cells versus B cells in clonal families at each age group (Figure 4a). Clonal family details in Supplementary Table 9 and network visualizations in Extended Data Figure 9a.

nodes.claim_text: Transcriptomic diversity analysis identified brain myeloid microglial cells and kidney macrophages as cell types with significant age-related changes in diversity.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA-seq (FACS Smart-seq2 and 10x Genomics droplet)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA-seq data from FACS (brain myeloid) and droplet (kidney) platforms across all age groups.
nodes.analysis_type: diversity score analysis across cell types and ages
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Raw FACS or droplet datasets for each tissue-cell type combination. For FACS brain myeloid microglial cells: n = 4,532 at 3 months, 4,461 at 18 months, 4,424 at 24 months (n = 14 mice total). For droplet kidney macrophages: n = 62 at 1 month, 139 at 3 months, 264 at 18 months, 105 at 21 months, 284 at 24 months, 553 at 30 months (n = 16 mice total).

PREPROCESSING: Genes expressed in fewer than 5 cells were filtered. Cells expressing fewer than 500 genes were filtered. Cells with total counts less than 5,000 were discarded. Size factor normalization was applied such that every cell had 10,000 counts, followed by log1p transformation.

CORE ANALYSIS: For every tissue-cell type combination, cells from each individual mouse were clustered separately using 6 different configurations: 3 resolution parameters (0.3, 0.5, 0.7) crossed with 2 clustering methods (Louvain and Leiden). For each mouse-level clustering result, the diversity score was computed as the Shannon entropy of the cluster assignment. The diversity score was then regressed against age using linear regression to detect systematic increase or decrease of clustering diversity with respect to age. FDR correction (Benjamini-Hochberg) was applied for multiple comparisons across all tissue-cell type combinations. A tissue-cell type was selected as significant if: (1) the slope was consistent (same sign) across all 6 clustering configurations, and (2) at least 2 out of 6 configurations had FDR < 0.3.

OUTPUT: Diversity scores per mouse for each clustering configuration, with regression P values (FDR-corrected). Heat map summaries of tissue-level and tissue-cell type-level diversity scores (Extended Data Figure 10). Detailed diversity score plots for brain myeloid microglial cells and kidney macrophages (Extended Data Figure 11a-b). Significant cell types identified in Figure 4c.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 4c; Extended Data Figure 10; Extended Data Figure 11a-b; Main text - diversity score (page 593-594)
nodes.supporting_quote: We observed significant changes in diversity for cells of the immune system that originate from the brain and the kidney (Fig. 4c, Extended Data Fig. 11a, b).

nodes.claim_text: The age-related diversity changes in brain microglia and kidney macrophages were not confounded by the number of genes expressed per cell.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA-seq (FACS Smart-seq2 and 10x Genomics droplet)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA-seq data from FACS (brain myeloid) and droplet (kidney) platforms across all age groups.
nodes.analysis_type: confound analysis for diversity score
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Diversity scores computed for all tissue-cell type combinations (as in L31) and the mean number of genes expressed per cell for each mouse-level clustering result.

PREPROCESSING: Same as L31.

CORE ANALYSIS: For each of the 6 clustering configurations (3 resolutions x 2 methods), the diversity score was plotted against the mean number of genes expressed per cell across all tissue-cell type-mouse combinations. Linear regression was performed to compute R2 values, assessing whether the number of genes detected per cell explains the variation in diversity scores. This was done separately at the tissue level and at the tissue-cell type level.

OUTPUT: Scatter plots of diversity score versus mean number of genes per cell for each clustering configuration, with linear regression R2 values. Shown in Extended Data Figure 11c (tissue level) and Extended Data Figure 11d (tissue-cell type level). Low R2 values indicate that gene detection rate does not confound the diversity score results.
nodes.evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 11c-d; Main text - diversity score (page 594)
nodes.supporting_quote: These results were not confounded by the number of genes expressed per cell (Extended Data Fig. 11c, d).

nodes.claim_text: In brain myeloid microglial cells, young (3-month) microglia predominantly occupied distinct transcriptomic clusters from old (18-month and 24-month) microglia.
nodes.experiment_detail: FACS-based single-cell RNA-seq on brain myeloid microglial cells from C57BL/6JN mice at 3, 18, and 24 months. n = 4,532, 4,461 and 4,424 individual microglia cells respectively.
nodes.analysis_type: cluster composition analysis by age
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: FACS data for brain myeloid microglial cells across 3 time points (n = 4,532 at 3 months, 4,461 at 18 months, 4,424 at 24 months).

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, cells with fewer than 5,000 counts removed (FACS). Data normalized to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformed. Highly variable genes computed, data scaled to maximum value of 10. PCA computed, followed by neighbourhood graph construction.

CORE ANALYSIS: All microglial cells from all ages were clustered together using the Leiden algorithm. A separate UMAP was computed using cells from all mice for visualization, using Leiden clustering with resolution parameter 0.7 (as specified in the Methods for the diversity score visualization). The age composition of each cluster was assessed by computing the proportion of cells from each age group within each cluster.

OUTPUT: UMAP plot of brain myeloid microglial cells with Leiden cluster numbers, colored by age (Figure 4d). Clusters 1 and 6 predominantly contain 3-month cells; clusters 10, 12, and 14 predominantly contain 18-month and 24-month cells.

nodes.supporting_quote: In brain myeloid microglial cells, the majority of young (3 month) microglia occupy clusters 1 and 6, whereas old (18 month and 24 month) microglia constitute the vast majority of cells in clusters 10, 12 and 14

nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: FACS data for brain myeloid microglial cells across 3 time points (n = 4,532 at 3 months, 4,461 at 18 months, 4,424 at 24 months), with Leiden cluster assignments.

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing as described for L33.

CORE ANALYSIS: Partition-based graph abstraction (PAGA) was used to reconstruct the ageing trajectory in brain microglial cells, as implemented in Scanpy (Wolf et al. 2019). PAGA infers connectivity between clusters and produces a topology-preserving map. Specific PAGA parameters are not stated in the paper beyond the use of the method itself.

OUTPUT: A trajectory graph showing the inferred progression from young (3-month-enriched) clusters through intermediate (18-month-enriched) clusters to old (24-month-enriched) clusters (Extended Data Figure 11e).
nodes.evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 11e; Main text - brain microglia (page 594)
nodes.supporting_quote: Trajectory analysis suggests that young microglia go through an intermediate state, represented by the clusters mostly occupied by 18-month microglial cells, before acquiring the signature of old microglia

nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: FACS data for brain myeloid microglial cells, with Leiden cluster assignments. Cells in clusters 10, 12, and 14 (predominantly 18-month and 24-month cells) were compared to cells in clusters 1 and 6 (predominantly 3-month cells).

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing as described for L33.

CORE ANALYSIS: Differential gene expression analysis was performed comparing old microglial clusters (10, 12, 14) to young microglial clusters (1, 6). The specific statistical method for this between-cluster comparison is not explicitly stated in the paper (the MAST framework with age as continuous covariate is described for tissue-level analyses; this cluster-based comparison may use a different approach). Genes upregulated in old clusters were identified.

OUTPUT: A list of differentially expressed genes between young and old microglial clusters, with MHC class I genes (H2-D1, H2-K1, B2m) and Fth1 among the upregulated genes in old clusters. Full gene list in Supplementary Table 10.

INPUT DATA: FACS data for brain myeloid microglial cells, with Leiden cluster assignments. Cells in clusters 10, 12, and 14 (predominantly 18-month and 24-month cells) were compared to cells in clusters 1 and 6 (predominantly 3-month cells).

nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: The top 200 differentially expressed genes between young microglial clusters (1, 6) and old microglial clusters (10, 12, 14) from the FACS brain myeloid dataset. The previously reported Alzheimer's disease-specific microglial signature from Keren-Shaul et al. 2017 (reference 47).

The overlap between the two gene lists was computed by counting the number of shared genes. The Alzheimer's disease signature and the healthy microglia signature were also scored across all microglial cells for UMAP visualization (Figure 4e).

nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Droplet data for kidney macrophages across 6 time points (n = 62 at 1 month, 139 at 3 months, 264 at 18 months, 105 at 21 months, 284 at 24 months, 553 at 30 months).

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, cells with fewer than 2,500 UMIs removed (droplet). nodes.claim_text: Changes in gene expression observed in bulk experiments are often attributable to both changes in the number of cells of a given type and changes in per-cell gene expression levels.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA-seq (FACS Smart-seq2 and 10x Genomics droplet)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA-seq data from FACS and droplet platforms were compared with bulk RNA-seq data from tissues (Schaum et al. 2019). Only male mice were used for comparison.
nodes.analysis_type: comparison of bulk and single-cell age-related gene expression changes
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Single-cell RNA-seq data from male mice only, from both FACS and droplet datasets. Bulk RNA-seq data from tissues (reference 9, Schaum et al. 2019 preprint). Analysis performed on a per-tissue basis.

PREPROCESSING - Single-cell: Only cells from male mice were retained. Count matrices were transformed using log(1 + counts per million (cpm)), with normalization done on a per-cell basis. Cells were divided into two age groups: young (age <= 3 months, denoted Y) and old (age > 3 months, denoted O).

PREPROCESSING - Bulk: Bulk RNA-seq data was normalized using DESeq2.

CORE ANALYSIS - Single-cell: For each gene, two metrics were computed: (1) Cell count: the fraction of cells expressing the gene (>0 counts). The log2 fold change of cell count between O and Y was computed. (2) Read count: the mean read count of the gene in cells that express it. The log2 fold change of read count between O and Y was computed.

CORE ANALYSIS - Bulk: For each gene, the Spearman correlation (rho) of DESeq2-normalized bulk gene expression with age was computed. Genes were classified into two groups: increasing with age (rho > 0.7, denoted U) and decreasing with age (rho < -0.7, denoted D).

CORE ANALYSIS - Comparison: The single-cell log2 fold changes (both cell count and read count) were compared between the bulk-defined groups U and D using the Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney test. The U statistic was used as the effect size. This assessed whether single-cell-based changes in cell count or read count could distinguish between genes that increase or decrease with age in bulk data.

OUTPUT: Scatter plots of read count-based effect size versus cell count-based effect size, and read count-based -log10(P value) versus cell count-based -log10(P value), for each tissue (Extended Data Figure 5a for FACS, 5b for droplet). Points are colored by tissue and categorized as: bulk signal explained by cell count only, read count only, both, or neither.
nodes.evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 5a-b; Main text - paragraph 3 (page 590)
nodes.supporting_quote: we observed that-in many cases-changes in gene expression are due to both changes in the numbers of cells in a population and changes in the gene expression levels in each cell

nodes.claim_text: B cells and endothelial cells independently annotated in different organs clustered together by unbiased whole-transcriptome analysis irrespective of tissue of origin or processing method.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA-seq (FACS Smart-seq2 and 10x Genomics droplet)
nodes.experiment_detail: All 356,213 cells from both FACS and droplet platforms were combined after batch correction using bbknn.
nodes.analysis_type: cross-tissue and cross-method clustering validation
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: All cells passing QC filters from both FACS (n = 110,824) and droplet (n = 245,389) datasets, with cell-type annotations assigned independently for each tissue.

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, method-specific count thresholds applied. Data normalized to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformed. Highly variable genes computed, data scaled to maximum value of 10. PCA computed. The bbknn batch-alignment algorithm (Polanski et al. 2020) was used in place of the standard neighbourhood graph computation to correct for method-associated batch effects between FACS and droplet data.

CORE ANALYSIS: After bbknn batch correction, all cells were clustered using Louvain graph-based clustering (Blondel et al. 2008). UMAP visualization was computed. The co-occurrence of cells with the same cell-type annotation (e.g., B cells from different tissues, endothelial cells from different tissues) in the same Louvain clusters was assessed, irrespective of tissue of origin or sequencing method.

OUTPUT: UMAP plots showing B cells and endothelial cells clustering together across tissues and methods (Figure 1e-f for combined data; Extended Data Figure 1g-h for FACS only; Extended Data Figure 1i-j for droplet only; Extended Data Figure 1k-l for cross-method).
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 1e-f; Extended Data Figure 1g-l
nodes.supporting_quote: cells annotated as B cells or endothelial cells tend to occupy the same clusters irrespective of their tissue of origin or the method with which they were processed

nodes.claim_text: More than 70% of cells in the dataset were automatically annotated using the Tabula Muris 3-month reference, with the remainder annotated manually by tissue experts.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA-seq (FACS Smart-seq2 and 10x Genomics droplet)
nodes.experiment_detail: Cell type annotation was performed on all cells across all age groups using the Tabula Muris 3-month data as reference.
nodes.analysis_type: semi-automated cell type annotation
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Single-cell RNA-seq data from all tissues across all age groups, from both FACS and droplet methods. The Tabula Muris (3-month time point) served as the reference dataset with existing cell-type annotations.

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing applied per tissue: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, method-specific count thresholds applied. Data normalized to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformed. Highly variable genes computed, data scaled.

CORE ANALYSIS: Each organ was analyzed independently, combining all ages. PCA was performed on the most variable genes. Louvain and Leiden graph-based clustering were applied. The data was then subset to 3-month cells (Tabula Muris), and the mapping between existing Tabula Muris cell-type labels and the clusters was computed. For clusters with a single 1:1 mapping (one cluster maps to exactly one cell type), the cell-type annotation was propagated to all cells in that cluster across all ages. For clusters with a 1:many mapping (one cluster maps to multiple cell types), the cluster was flagged for manual validation. All automated annotations were reviewed by tissue expert curators. Remaining unannotated cells were annotated manually by hand. Annotations were provided in the controlled vocabulary of the Cell Ontology (Diehl et al. 2016). The automated annotation step took approximately 1 minute per tissue.

OUTPUT: Cell-type annotations for all cells in the dataset. More than 70% of cells were automatically annotated; the remainder were manually curated. Annotation workflow schematic shown in Figure 1b.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 1b; Main text - paragraph 2 (page 590)
nodes.supporting_quote: Using this approach, we were able to automatically annotate more than 70% of the cells. All the automated cell annotations were reviewed and approved by human experts, and the remaining cells were annotated by hand

nodes.claim_text: The relative abundance of precursor B cells in bone marrow decreased significantly with age.
nodes.experiment_detail: Droplet-based single-cell RNA-seq on bone marrow cells from C57BL/6JN mice at 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months. n = 3,027, 8,559, 11,496, 5,216, 12,943 and 13,496 cells respectively.
nodes.analysis_type: cell type proportion analysis across age
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: All cells passing QC filters from bone marrow across available time points (n = 3,027 at 1 month, 8,559 at 3 months, 11,496 at 18 months, 5,216 at 21 months, 12,943 at 24 months, 13,496 at 30 months), with cell-type annotations.

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, cells with fewer than 2,500 UMIs removed (droplet). Data normalized to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformed.

CORE ANALYSIS: For each age group, the relative proportion of precursor B cells in bone marrow was computed as (number of precursor B cells) / (total number of cells in marrow at that age). Linear regression of relative cell-type proportion against age was performed using scipy.stats linregress. Significance was assessed with P < 0.05 for a hypothesis test with the null hypothesis that the slope is zero, using a two-sided Wald test with t-distribution of the test statistic, and r2 > 0.7.

OUTPUT: Scatter plot of relative precursor B cell proportion versus age in bone marrow, with regression line (Extended Data Figure 5e). P value and r2 value reported in Supplementary Table 5.
nodes.evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 5e; Supplementary Table 5
nodes.supporting_quote: Relative abundances of marrow precursor B cells change significantly with age

nodes.analysis_detail:

CORE ANALYSIS: For each age group, the relative proportion of keratinocyte stem cells in skin was computed as (number of keratinocyte stem cells) / (total number of cells in skin at that age). Linear regression of relative cell-type proportion against age was performed using scipy.stats linregress. Significance was assessed with P < 0.05 for a hypothesis test with the null hypothesis that the slope is zero, using a two-sided Wald test with t-distribution of the test statistic, and r2 > 0.7.

OUTPUT: Scatter plot of relative keratinocyte stem cell proportion versus age in skin, with regression line (Extended Data Figure 5g). P value and r2 value reported in Supplementary Table 5.

nodes.claim_text: Hepatocyte cell size increased and cell number decreased with age in the liver as shown by bright-field imaging.
nodes.experiment_detail: Bright-field imaging of hepatocytes from C57BL/6JN mice at 3, 12, 18, and 24 months. Cell size and cell number were quantified.
nodes.analysis_type: image-based quantification of cell size and number
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Bright-field images of liver tissue from mice at 3, 12, 18, and 24 months of age. Beta-catenin staining was used to visualize cell boundaries.

PREPROCESSING: Liver tissue was prepared for bright-field imaging. Beta-catenin staining was performed to delineate hepatocyte boundaries (as shown in Extended Data Figure 6b).

CORE ANALYSIS: Hepatocyte cell size (in micrometers) and cell number per field of view were quantified from bright-field images at each age group (3, 12, 18, 24 months). Statistical comparisons between age groups were performed using Welch's test (pairwise comparisons between all age groups).

OUTPUT: Box plots of cell size and cell number at each age group with P values from Welch's test (Extended Data Figure 6c for cell size, Extended Data Figure 6d for cell number). Sample sizes for each group are not explicitly stated in the figure legend but data points are shown.
nodes.evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 6b-d
nodes.supporting_quote: Bright-field imaging of hepatocytes at different ages (b) and respective quantification (c, d).

nodes.claim_text: Albumin (Alb) expression in the liver decreased with age, consistent with the declining proportion of hepatocytes.
nodes.experiment_detail: Droplet-based single-cell RNA-seq on liver cells from C57BL/6JN mice at 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months.
nodes.analysis_type: differential gene expression analysis with age as continuous covariate
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: All cells passing QC filters from the liver across available time points (n = 2,791 at 1 month, 2,832 at 3 months, 3,806 at 18 months, 2,257 at 21 months, 6,384 at 24 months, 5,713 at 30 months).

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, cells with fewer than 2,500 UMIs removed (droplet). Data normalized to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformed.

CORE ANALYSIS: Differential gene expression analysis was performed on the liver tissue using MAST, treating age as a continuous numerical covariate while controlling for sex and technology as additional covariates. Genes were classified as significantly differentially expressed under FDR < 0.01 and age coefficient threshold of 0.005 (corresponding to approximately 10% fold change).

OUTPUT: A ranked list of differentially expressed genes with age coefficients. Alb (albumin) appears among the top 10 downregulated genes in Extended Data Figure 6e. Full gene list in Supplementary Table 6.
nodes.evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 6e; Main text - liver composition (page 593); Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: We observed an age-related decrease in the relative number of hepatocytes (Extended Data Fig. 6a-d), which is supported by the reduction in the expression of albumin (Alb; Extended Data Fig. 6e, Supplementary Table 6).

nodes.claim_text: Immune response genes Tnfrsf12a and Cdkn1a were overexpressed with age in both bladder mesenchymal cells and bladder urothelial cells.
nodes.experiment_detail: Droplet-based single-cell RNA-seq on bladder cells from C57BL/6JN mice at 1, 3, 18, and 24 months.
nodes.analysis_type: cell-type-specific differential gene expression across age
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Bladder mesenchymal cells and bladder urothelial cells (separately) passing QC filters across available time points (1, 3, 18, 24 months). Total bladder cell numbers: n = 970 at 1 month, 3,804 at 3 months, 2,739 at 18 months, 3,864 at 24 months.

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, cells with fewer than 2,500 UMIs removed (droplet). Data normalized to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformed. Cells were subset to bladder mesenchymal cells or bladder urothelial cells for cell-type-specific analyses.

CORE ANALYSIS: Differential gene expression was performed separately within bladder mesenchymal cells and within bladder urothelial cells using MAST, treating age as a continuous numerical covariate while controlling for sex and technology. Genes were classified as significant under FDR < 0.01 and age coefficient threshold of 0.005 (corresponding to approximately 10% fold change). The age coefficients for Tnfrsf12a and Cdkn1a were assessed in each cell type.

OUTPUT: Age coefficients for Tnfrsf12a and Cdkn1a within bladder mesenchymal cells and bladder urothelial cells. Results reported in Supplementary Table 6.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text - bladder composition (page 591); Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: the overexpression of genes associated with immune response-such as Tnfrsf12a and Cdkn1a-in both bladder (mesenchymal) cells and bladder urothelial cells

nodes.claim_text: The age-related increase in somatic mutations was observed across all five functional cell-type groups (endothelial, immune, parenchymal, stem/progenitor, and stromal cells).
nodes.experiment_detail: Full-length transcript single-cell RNA-seq data from FACS Smart-seq2 across all analysed organs at 3, 18, and 24 months. Cell types were grouped into five functional categories.
nodes.analysis_type: somatic mutation analysis stratified by cell-type functional group
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: Mutation count per cell tables generated from the GATK HaplotypeCaller pipeline applied to all FACS samples, as described in L24. Cell-type annotations were used to stratify cells into five functional groups: endothelial cells, immune cells, parenchymal cells, stem/progenitor cells, and stromal cells.

PREPROCESSING: Same as L24: genes mutated in >60% of cells removed to eliminate germline mutation bias. For each tissue, genes expressed in at least 75% of cells at all time points were selected.

CORE ANALYSIS: The average number of mutations per gene per cell was computed for each age group, stratified by the five functional cell-type groups. The pairwise difference of mean mutations between age groups (24 months minus 3 months, and 18 months minus 3 months) was computed separately for each functional group. The same analysis was performed on ERCC spike-in controls for comparison. Average raw counts were also computed to control for sequencing coverage.

OUTPUT: Distributions of pairwise mean mutation differences for each of the five functional cell-type groups, shown separately for gene sets (filled distributions) and ERCC controls (dashed distributions) in Figure 3b-f. Extended Data Figures 7 and 8 show per-tissue breakdowns.
nodes.evidence_location: Figure 3b-f; Extended Data Figure 7
nodes.supporting_quote: Distribution of the difference of the mean mutation in the gene set (and ERCC spike-in controls) per cell between 24 months and 3 months and between 18 months and 3 months for all tissues and cells (a) and with the cell types split into five functional groups: endothelial cells (b), immune cells (c), parenchymal cells (d), stem/progenitor cells (e) and stromal cells (f).

nodes.claim_text: Senescence-associated genes E2f2, Lmnb1, Tnf, and Itgax showed positive correlation between ageing and the fraction of cells expressing them.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA-seq (FACS Smart-seq2 and 10x Genomics droplet)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA-seq data from all age groups across all tissues.
nodes.analysis_type: correlation of senescence marker expression fraction with age
nodes.analysis_detail:
INPUT DATA: All cells passing QC filters from both FACS and droplet datasets across all age groups. A curated list of previously characterized senescence markers compiled from references 12-15 (provided in Supplementary Table 4), which includes E2f2, Lmnb1, Tnf, and Itgax.

PREPROCESSING: Standard preprocessing: genes not expressed in at least 3 cells removed, cells with fewer than 250 detected genes removed, method-specific count thresholds applied (5,000 for FACS, 2,500 UMIs for droplet). Data normalized to 10,000 counts per cell and log-transformed.

CORE ANALYSIS: For each gene in the curated senescence marker list (Supplementary Table 4), the fraction of cells expressing the gene (>0 counts) was computed per age group across all tissues. The correlation between age and the fraction of cells expressing each senescence marker was computed. Genes with positive correlation were identified.

OUTPUT: Correlation values between age and fraction of expressing cells for each senescence marker. E2f2, Lmnb1, Tnf, and Itgax showed positive correlations. Results plotted in Supplementary Table 4.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text - paragraph 3 (page 591); Supplementary Table 4
nodes.supporting_quote: other genes with positive correlation include E2f2, Lmnb1, Tnf and Itgax.
